subject: Computer Basics
question_text: In the computer memory hierarchy, which type of memory is the fastest but has the smallest capacity?

options.text: Main Memory (RAM)
Secondary Storage
CPU Registers
Cache Memory

step_by_step: Registers are at the top of the hierarchy (inside the CPU).
Followed by Cache (L1, L2, L3), then RAM, then Secondary Storage.

subject: Computer Basics
question_text: What is the primary purpose of 'Cache Memory' in a computer system?

options.text: To increase the capacity of RAM
To act as a permanent storage for OS files
To bridge the speed gap between the CPU and Main Memory
To store the BIOS settings

solution.final_answer: To bridge the speed gap between the CPU and Main Memory

step_by_step: CPUs are much faster than RAM.
Cache stores frequently used data closer to the CPU to avoid 'wait states'.

subject: Computer Basics
question_text: Which level of Cache is usually integrated directly into the CPU core and is the fastest?

options.text: Level 1 (L1)
Level 2 (L2)
Level 3 (L3)
L4 Cache

step_by_step: L3 is larger but shared across cores and slightly slower.

options.text: Retains data permanently
Loses its contents when the power is turned off
Is used for long-term backups
Cannot be rewritten

solution.final_answer: Loses its contents when the power is turned off

step_by_step: HDD, SSD, and ROM are non-volatile.

options.text: A type of physical RAM chip
A portion of the hard drive treated as RAM by the OS
Memory used by graphics cards only
A backup server in the cloud

solution.final_answer: A portion of the hard drive treated as RAM by the OS

step_by_step: Virtual memory allows a computer to run larger programs than its physical RAM capacity by swapping data between RAM and the hard disk.

subject: Computer Basics
question_text: Which of the following describes the relationship between distance from CPU and cost per byte in the memory hierarchy?

options.text: As distance increases, cost per byte increases
As distance increases, cost per byte decreases
Distance and cost are unrelated
All memory levels cost the same

solution.final_answer: As distance increases, cost per byte decreases

step_by_step: Hard drives (furthest) are cheapest per GB.

subject: Computer Basics
question_text: Static RAM (SRAM) is typically used for:

options.text: Main memory (RAM)
Cache memory
Hard drive storage
BIOS storage

step_by_step: SRAM is faster and does not need refreshing, making it perfect for cache.
DRAM (Dynamic RAM) is cheaper and used for main system memory.

options.text: Physical damage to a RAM stick
Failure to release memory that is no longer needed by a program
Data being stolen from a computer
Electricity leaking from the motherboard

solution.final_answer: Failure to release memory that is no longer needed by a program

step_by_step: If a program keeps requesting RAM but never releases it, the system eventually runs out of memory.

subject: Computer Basics
question_text: In memory organization, the principle of 'Locality of Reference' suggests that:

options.text: A computer only uses memory located near the CPU
A program tends to access a small portion of its address space at any given time
All data is equally likely to be accessed
Memory should be located near the power supply

solution.final_answer: A program tends to access a small portion of its address space at any given time

step_by_step: Temporal Locality: Recently accessed items will be accessed again soon.
Spatial Locality: Items near recently accessed items will likely be accessed soon.

subject: Computer Basics
question_text: Wait states in a CPU occur when:

options.text: The computer is in sleep mode
The CPU must wait for data to be delivered from slower memory (like RAM)
The user is not typing anything
A video is buffering

solution.final_answer: The CPU must wait for data to be delivered from slower memory (like RAM)

step_by_step: Wait states reduce overall system performance.
